perturbation.original_text: FASC shall create and maintain all necessary books and records in accordance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations, including but not limited to records required by Section 31(a) of the 1940 Act and the rules thereunder, as the same may be amended from time to time, pertaining to the Services performed by it and not otherwise created and maintained by another party. Where applicable, such records shall be maintained by FASC for the periods and in the places required by Rule 31a-2 under the 1940 Act.
perturbation.changed_text: FASC shall create and maintain some books and records as it deems necessary and only to the extent that they are convenient to create, without reference to Section 31(a) of the 1940 Act and the rules thereunder. Such records shall be maintained by FASC for a duration determined at its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires FASC to maintain records as required by Section 31(a) of the 1940 Act and related rules. The modified text makes record-keeping discretionary and removes any obligation to comply with the 1940 Act. This creates uncertainty in enforcement as it deviates from legally mandated record-keeping requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Investment Company Act of 1940, Section 31(a) and Rule 31a-2
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the Investment Company Act of 1940, specifically Section 31(a) and Rule 31a-2, which mandate specific record-keeping requirements for investment companies. The modified text removes these requirements, making the contract non-compliant with federal law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 270.31a-2 - Records to be preserved by registered investment companies, certain majority-owned subsidiaries thereof, and other persons having transactions with registered investment companies. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 270.31a-2 - Records to be preserved by registered investment companies, certain majority-owned subsidiaries thereof, and other persons having transactions with registered investment companies. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 270.31a-2 Records to be preserved by registered investment companies, certain majority-owned subsidiaries thereof, and other persons having transactions with registered investment companies. (a) Every registered investment company shall: (1) Preserve permanently, the first two years in an easily accessible place, all books and records required to be made pursuant to paragraphs (1) through (4) of § 270.31a-1(b) ; (2) Preserve for a period not less than six years from the end of the fiscal year in which any transaction occurred, the first two years in an easily accessible place, all books and records required to be made pursuant to § 270.31a-1(b)(5) through (12) and all vouchers, memoranda, correspondence, checkbooks, bank statements, cancelled checks, cash reconciliation, cancelled stock certificates, and all schedules evidencing and supporting each computation of net asset value of the investment company shares , including schedules evidencing and supporting each computation of an adjustment to net asset value of the investment company shares based on swing pricing policies and procedures established and implemented pursuant to § 270.22c-1(a)(3) , all schedules evidencing and supporting each computation of a liquidity fee by a money market fund pursuant to § 270.2a-7(c)(2) , and other documents required to be maintained by § 270.31a-1(a) and not enumerated in § 270.31a-1(b) . (3) Preserve for
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts Section 31(a) of the 1940 Act and Rule 31a-2, as stated in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. The original text requires compliance with these regulations, while the changed text makes record-keeping discretionary and removes the obligation to comply with the 1940 Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 17 CFR § 270.31a-2, stating that 'Every registered investment company shall...Preserve permanently...all books and records required to be made pursuant to paragraphs (1) through (4) of § 270.31a-1(b)'. By making record-keeping discretionary, the changed text allows FASC to avoid preserving records as mandated by law, resulting in a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, to the extent that the Services to be provided with respect to any Account which is registered as an investment company under the 1940 Act (herein referred to as a "registered investment company") are services referred to in the definition of "investment advisor" under Section 202(a)(11) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 (herein referred to as "investment advisory services"), then with respect to such Account, this Agreement: (i) shall not commence until the effective date of its approval by the board of directors or trustees ("Board") of such Account; (ii) shall continue from year to year thereafter, subject to the provisions for termination and all other terms and conditions hereof, only if such continuation shall be specifically approved at least annually by a majority of the Board, including a majority of the members of the Board who are not parties to this Agreement or interested persons of any such party (other than as members of the Board) cast in person at a meeting called for that purpose
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, to the extent that the Services to be provided with respect to any Account which is registered as an investment company under the 1940 Act (herein referred to as a "registered investment company") are services referred to in the definition of "investment advisor" under Section 202(a)(11) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 (herein referred to as "investment advisory services"), then with respect to such Account, this Agreement: (i) shall commence immediately; (ii) shall continue indefinitely, unless otherwise determined by FASC, without requiring approval by the board of directors or trustees.
perturbation.explanation: The original text correctly states that the agreement requires approval by the board of directors or trustees of the investment company for it to commence and continue. The modified text removes this requirement, allowing the agreement to commence immediately and continue indefinitely without board approval, contradicting the Investment Company Act of 1940.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires that investment advisory contracts be approved by the board of directors or trustees of the investment company. The modified text eliminates this requirement, directly contradicting the law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has been approved by the vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such registered company, and— (1) precisely describes all compensation to be paid thereunder; (2) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance is specifically approved at least annually by the board of directors or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company; (3) provides, in substance, that it may be terminated at any time, without the payment of any penalty, by the board of directors of such registered company or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company on not more than sixty days’ written notice to the investment adviser ; and (4) provides, in substance, for its automatic termination in the event of its assignment . (b) Written contract with company for sale by principal underwriter of security of which company is issuer; contents It shall be unlawful for any principal underwriter for a registered open-end company to offer for sale, sell, or deliver after sale any security of which such company is the issuer, except pursuant to a written contract with such company, which contract— (1) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance
perturbation.justification: The modified text eliminates the requirement for board approval of the investment advisory contract, which is a direct violation of Section 15(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 (a)(2), states that the contract shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance is specifically approved at least annually by the board of directors. By removing this approval requirement, the modified contract directly contradicts the U.S. Code, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be amended at any time by mutual written agreement of the parties hereto; provided, however, that no Amendment to this Agreement shall be effective with respect to any investment advisory services to be provided to any Account which is registered investment company unless, to the extent required by Section 15(a)(2) of the 1940 Act, such amendment has been approved both by the vote of a majority of the Board of the Account, including a majority of the members of the Board who are not parties to this Agreement or interested persons of any such party (other than as members of the Board), cast in person at a meeting called for that purpose and, where required by Section 15(a)(2) of the 1940 Act, on behalf of the Account by a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such Account as defined in Section 2(a)(42) of the 1940 Act.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended at any time by mutual written agreement of the parties hereto; Amendments can be made without requiring approval of the board, and shareholders cannot change amendments made.
perturbation.explanation: The original text correctly states that any amendment to the agreement that affects investment advisory services must be approved by the board of directors and, where required by Section 15(a)(2) of the 1940 Act, the shareholders of the investment company. The modified text removes this requirement and specifically states that Amendments can be made without requiring approval of the board and shareholders. This contradicts the Investment Company Act of 1940.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(a)(2) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires that any material amendment to an investment advisory contract must be approved by both the board of directors and the shareholders. The modified text directly contradicts this requirement, making it unenforceable.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has been approved by the vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such registered company, and— (1) precisely describes all compensation to be paid thereunder; (2) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance is specifically approved at least annually by the board of directors or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company; (3) provides, in substance, that it may be terminated at any time, without the payment of any penalty, by the board of directors of such registered company or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company on not more than sixty days’ written notice to the investment adviser ; and (4) provides, in substance, for its automatic termination in the event of its assignment . (b) Written contract with company for sale by principal underwriter of security of which company is issuer; contents It shall be unlawful for any principal underwriter for a registered open-end company to offer for sale, sell, or deliver after sale any security of which such company is the issuer, except pursuant to a written contract with such company, which contract— (1) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts Section 15(a)(2) of the Investment Company Act of 1940, which mandates that material amendments to investment advisory contracts must be approved by both the board of directors and the shareholders. The changed text explicitly states that "Amendments can be made without requiring approval of the board, and shareholders cannot change amendments made." This is in direct opposition to the legal requirement for board and shareholder approval. Scraped Law Snippet 2 provides support, referencing 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15, which concerns contracts of advisers and underwriters and implies the necessity of adhering to specific regulations, further strengthening the assertion of a legal contradiction.
